sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hypermobility presentations often involve recurrent pain, instability, fatigue, and poor load tolerance. Screening should recognise this pattern while also watching for dysautonomia, widespread pain, and safety concerns.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Recurrent sprains, subluxation feelings, or multiple pain sites
- Joint instability, fatigue, or poor recovery after activity
- Soft tissue injury with seemingly minor loads
- Long history of flexibility or clumsiness

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Hypermobility does not mean every symptom is benign or that all pain should be dismissed as laxity
- Consider dysautonomia, chronic pain, pelvic floor issues, and pregnancy-related implications in more complex cases
- Generalised hypermobility needs load-building, not repeated aggressive stretching

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Identify whether the current issue is stable overload or an acute unsafe instability or neurological presentation
- Advise controlled movement and simple support strategies rather than end-range provocation
- Escalate for significant neurological findings, syncope, or major functional decline

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Assess symptom behaviour, instability pattern, fatigue, and broader multisystem history where relevant
- Use progressive strength, motor control, pacing, and confidence-focused rehab rather than instability-focused fear messaging
- Refer for rheumatology, genetics, pelvic health, or broader multidisciplinary review when the pattern is complex or disabling

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Hypermobile patients often need steadiness, dosage, and consistency more than intensity
